ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tea beverage class; no specific class for tea with milk foam exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers prepared tea beverages; no specific foam-topped tea subcode exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for tea with milk foam; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: topped with whipped or frothed milk foam

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of tea with foam is approximately 250 ml (one cup), equivalent to roughly 250 g, as commonly served at Singaporean and Malaysian beverage stalls and cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Tea with foam — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient composition data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values preserved as reported. Saturated fat estimated from fat composition of milk foam component using HPB and USDA SR Legacy data for whole milk foam (~62% of total fat as saturated fat), yielding approximately 2.41 g saturated fat per 100 g. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and other micronutrient fields set to -1 where not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tea with milk foam as a composite beverage. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the dominant glycemic driver being sucrose/added sugar (GI ~65) diluted by the low-GI tea base and fat-containing milk foam which slows gastric emptying. This is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened milk tea analogues. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (55 × 10.78 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 14.8 g GL per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (7.86 g/100 g) in a beverage format independently elevates blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 67 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage (7.86 g sugar/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold) as dietary sugar independently raises blood pressure via insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation (AHA 2018); request unsweetened tea with foam instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.86 g sugar per 100 g; liquid sugar causes rapid postprandial glucose spike regardless of quantity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage as it is a sugar-sweetened drink delivering approximately 19.7 g of sugar per 250 ml serving, causing rapid glucose absorption and postprandial hyperglycaemia; unsweetened tea with foam (zero added sugar) is the only diabetes-compatible modification.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from milk foam (~2.41 g/100 g) and added sugar (7.86 g/100 g) both contribute to cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this beverage due to its saturated fat content from milk foam and high added sugar, both of which are associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and triglycerides; choosing unsweetened tea with minimal or non-dairy low-fat foam reduces cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (7.86 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; saturated fat from milk foam may exacerbate systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Persons with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage as dietary sugar promotes inflammatory mediators including TNF-α and IL-6; unsweetened black tea with minimal foam is preferred to retain tea polyphenol anti-inflammatory benefits without the sugar-driven inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tea with foam is a popular beverage in Singapore and Malaysia consisting of brewed black tea topped with a thick, creamy layer of whipped or frothed milk foam. The tea base is typically sweetened with sugar or syrup, while the foam layer — made from fresh milk, non-dairy creamer, or a combination — provides a rich, velvety mouthfeel that contrasts with the slightly bitter, tannin-forward tea beneath. The drink is commonly served cold or at room temperature and is widely available at hawker centres, kopitiam stalls, and bubble tea shops across the region. Nutritionally, tea with foam provides 81 kcal per 100 g, with 10.78 g carbohydrate (of which 7.86 g is sugar), 3.89 g fat (approximately 2.41 g saturated), 0.61 g protein, 0.92 g dietary fibre, and 67 mg sodium. The high sugar content classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard sweetened form. The milk foam contributes saturated fat, which is relevant for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. Black tea polyphenols (theaflavins, thearubigins) provide modest antioxidant activity, but these benefits are outweighed by the added sugar in the standard preparation. Unsweetened versions with minimal foam represent a significantly healthier alternative. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), with a glycemic load of approximately 14.8 per 250 ml serving.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for all fields. Saturated fat estimated from milk foam fat composition using HPB and USDA SR Legacy data for whole milk, which is a well-characterised ingredient. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database sweetened milk tea analogues, documented transparently. All other -1 fields reflect genuine absence of data in the HPB source for this composite beverage.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite tea-with-foam beverage are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. Saturated fat (2.41 g/100 g) is estimated from the milk foam fat fraction using USDA SR Legacy whole milk fat composition data (~62% saturated of total fat) and is flagged as an estimate. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tea with foam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.346 from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this was rejected as a forbidden/unsupported source and replaced with the analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Input health_suitability used condition key 'diabetes'; this has been remapped to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. Dietary fibre of 0.92 g/100 g is noted as unusual for a beverage but is preserved from the HPB source, possibly reflecting soluble fibre from tea solids or non-dairy creamer components.

Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for a GI value of 55, as per standard GI classification (GI 55 or less is low).
